text: We describe here the mini-invasive technique of laparoscopic myomectomy in case of a ten centimeters uterine myoma. Uterine exposure by cannulation is generally necessary, particularly in case of posterior myoma and entubation of the uterus.
The surgeon performs a vertical hystereotomy near the midline using either a monopolar needle or harmonic scalpel, carefully avoiding vascular areas and the fallopian tube.

The surgeon performs intracapsular enucleation of the uterine myoma, carefully dissecting the plane between the myoma and the myometrium using scissors, a monopolar needle, or a harmonic scalpel. Minimal coagulation is applied to prevent necrosis at the incision edges.

The surgeon inspects the surgical site to ensure the endometrial cavity remains closed following enucleation. If the cavity is open, it is sutured to prevent hemorrhagic complications and reduce the risk of intrauterine adhesions.

The surgeon closes a large myometrial defect using a two-layer suture technique, performing intracorporeal knots with absorbable suture material. The edges of the defect are carefully re-approximated, with figurinate sutures reserved for hemorrhagic conditions.
The surgeon continues to perform cross stitches with absorbable suture on the uterine wall, demonstrating the option to use a running suture with Veloc or StrataFix materials.
The surgeon inserts a containment bag into the surgical field for morcellation and extraction of the myoma, demonstrating a quick and safe technique.

The surgeon encounters difficulty during morcellation due to cystic or hydropic transformation of the myoma. An anti-adhesion agent is applied to the scar to minimize adhesion formation post-operation.
